section.title: Do Not Neglect Such a Salvation
section.sectionRationale: The first of the letter's five warning passages. Having proved the Son's supremacy, the writer draws the urgent 'therefore': if a word given through angels was binding, how much more the salvation announced by the Lord Himself. The danger is not violent rebellion but quiet drifting.
section.thesis: Because the Son is so superior to the angels, the salvation He announced carries far greater weight than the law given through angels; to neglect it is to drift toward inescapable judgment, so we must pay all the closer attention.
section.frameNote: A single argument from lesser to greater: the reliability of the message spoken by angels, then the far greater accountability for the salvation announced by the Lord, confirmed by eyewitnesses and attested by God with signs.

verses.text: We must pay closer attention, therefore, to what we have heard, so that we do not {{pararreo|drift away}}.
For if the message spoken by angels was {{bebaios|binding}}, and every transgression and disobedience received its just punishment,

units.label: So great a salvation, so confirmed

verses.text: how shall we escape if we neglect such a great {{soteria|salvation}}? This salvation was first announced by the Lord, was confirmed to us by those who heard Him,
and was affirmed by God through signs, wonders, various miracles, and gifts of the Holy Spirit distributed according to His will.

pararreo.gloss: To drift, slip, or flow past, like a boat carried off course or a ring slipping from a finger. The danger the writer names is not a deliberate leap into apostasy but a slow, unnoticed drifting away from what has been heard.

bebaios.gloss: Firm, reliable, legally binding and valid. The law given through angels was binding, and its violations were justly punished; the argument runs from the lesser binding word to the greater and weightier salvation in the Son.

soteria.gloss: Salvation, rescue, deliverance. Here it is 'so great a salvation', announced by the Lord Himself, confirmed by eyewitnesses, and attested by God with signs and gifts of the Spirit. Its greatness makes neglecting it all the more perilous.

basic.context: This is the letter's first warning. The writer has shown that the Son is greater than the angels who mediated the law at Sinai. Now he presses the point home: if breaking the law given through angels brought just punishment, how much more serious to ignore the salvation the Lord Himself announced. The peril is drifting, slowly slipping away, rather than openly rejecting.
basic.hermeneutics: Read this as pastoral warning, not cold threat. The 'we' includes the writer; it is a family being urged to hold on. The argument moves from lesser to greater: greater messenger, greater message, greater accountability.
basic.originalLanguages: The key verb is pararreō, to drift away like a ship off course. The law was bebaios, binding and valid. The gospel is so great a sōtēria, salvation, that to neglect it is to court a judgment none can escape.
basic.typology: The word at Sinai, mediated through angels, was a shadow of the greater word now spoken by the Lord. The eyewitnesses and confirming signs echo how God authenticated His word in earlier ages, now surpassed in the confirmation of the gospel.
basic.christ: Christ is the Lord who first announced this salvation. What the prophets and angels foreshadowed, He proclaimed in person, and His message is confirmed by those who heard Him and by God's own attesting power.
basic.god: God is just: transgression rightly receives its penalty. God is also generous, confirming the gospel with signs, wonders, miracles, and gifts of the Spirit, so that we have every reason to hold fast rather than drift.
basic.crossReferences: Hebrews 12 warns against refusing Him who speaks. Deuteronomy honours the binding law given at Sinai. Acts records the signs and wonders that confirmed the apostles' witness.
basic.oneStory: God's saving word runs through the whole Bible, growing in clarity until the Lord Himself announces salvation. This warning calls each generation not to let that word slip past unheeded.

context.literary: The passage is the first of the letter's alternating pattern of exposition and exhortation. It opens with 'therefore', drawing a practical conclusion from chapter 1, and argues in a single 'how much more' movement from the law to the gospel.
context.historical: The readers, likely Jewish believers under pressure, were tempted to slip back to familiar worship. The reference to eyewitnesses and confirming signs points to a second generation who received the gospel from those who had heard the Lord directly.
context.canonical: The warning stands with the letter's other four warning passages and echoes the wider biblical theme that greater revelation brings greater responsibility, as in the Gospels' warnings to those who witnessed Christ's works.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To urge the readers to attend all the more closely to the gospel they have heard, lest they drift and fall under a judgment more severe than that which befell lawbreakers under Moses.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage is directly prescriptive: pay closer attention, do not drift, do not neglect. The abiding command is vigilant, active attention to the gospel already received.

debates.question: Can a true believer 'drift away' and be lost?

views.label: A real warning God uses to preserve His own
views.case: Scripture teaches that God keeps His people, yet He does so partly through such warnings; the elect heed them and persevere, and the warning is one of the means by which they are kept from drifting.

views.label: Evidence that some professing believers were never truly saved
views.case: Those who drift and finally fall show they did not truly belong; genuine faith holds fast, and the warning exposes empty profession.

debates.bearing: On any faithful reading the command is the same: take the warning to heart and hold fast to Christ; presuming on grace while drifting is exactly the danger named.

study.originalLanguages: The passage hinges on a vivid image: pararreō, to drift or flow past, the picture of a vessel that slips its mooring and is carried away while no one notices. The law spoken through angels was bebaios, firm and legally binding, and its breach met a just recompense. Against that stands so great a sōtēria, salvation, announced by the Lord, confirmed by those who heard, and co-attested by God through signs, wonders, and varied gifts of the Spirit. The greatness of the salvation and the weight of its confirmation together sharpen the warning: to neglect such a word is to have no escape.

typology.type: The word given through angels at Sinai
typology.fulfillment: A binding but preparatory word, surpassed by the greater salvation the Lord Himself announced.

typology.type: God's attesting signs in Israel's history
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled and heightened in the signs, wonders, and gifts of the Spirit that confirmed the gospel.

study.christ: Christ is the Lord through whom this great salvation was first announced. He is greater than the angels who gave the law, and so His word carries greater weight. The salvation He proclaimed was confirmed by those who heard Him, the eyewitnesses of His ministry, and sealed by God's own attesting power. To neglect Christ's salvation is therefore the gravest of dangers, for there is no greater word to fall back upon and no other name by which to be saved.
study.god: God is shown as both just and gracious. His justice is seen in the truth that every transgression under the law received its due penalty, a principle that still stands. His grace is seen in the lengths to which He went to confirm the gospel: the Lord announced it, eyewitnesses attested it, and God Himself bore witness by signs, wonders, miracles, and gifts of the Holy Spirit distributed according to His will. The God who confirms so richly is the God who must not be ignored.

crossReferences.note: See that you do not refuse Him who speaks; the same warning against turning from God's word.
The law given amid the heavenly ones at Sinai, the binding word spoken through angels.
Jesus attested by God with miracles, wonders, and signs, matching God's confirmation of the salvation.
The Lord confirming the message by accompanying signs, echoing verse 4.

study.oneStory: From Sinai to the coming of the Lord, God has been speaking a saving word, each stage more glorious than the last. Now the Lord Himself has announced salvation and God has confirmed it beyond doubt. The single question this passage puts to every hearer in the ongoing story is whether we will attend to that word and hold fast, or let it quietly slip past until we drift beyond its reach.

quiz.q: What is the specific danger this warning names?

options.text: Drifting away, slowly slipping past what we have heard.
options.note: The verb pictures a boat carried off course, an unnoticed drift.
The peril here is quiet neglect and drift, not dramatic blasphemy.

options.text: Forgetting a few minor details of doctrine.
options.note: It is more serious than lost details; it is drifting from the gospel itself.

options.text: Being physically far from a church building.
options.note: The drift is spiritual, away from the message, not a matter of geography.

quiz.q: How does the writer argue that neglecting the gospel is so serious?

options.text: From lesser to greater: if breaking the law brought just punishment, neglecting the greater salvation is worse.
options.note: Verses 2 to 3 reason from the binding law to the greater salvation.

options.text: By saying the law never mattered at all.
options.note: The law was binding and just; the point is that the gospel is greater still.

options.text: By promising there are no consequences either way.
options.note: The warning is precisely about inescapable consequences.

options.text: By claiming the gospel is easier to ignore safely.
options.note: The opposite: it is far more dangerous to ignore, being so great a salvation.

quiz.q: How was this great salvation confirmed?

options.text: Announced by the Lord, confirmed by those who heard Him, and attested by God with signs and gifts of the Spirit.
options.note: Verses 3 to 4 list the layers of confirmation.
It was confirmed by eyewitnesses and by God Himself.
It rested first on the Lord and eyewitnesses, attested by God.

options.text: By the angels who gave the law.
options.note: The angels gave the law; this salvation was announced by the Lord Himself.

quiz.q: What is the right response to this warning?

options.text: Pay closer attention to the gospel and hold fast, so as not to drift.
Assume grace covers all neglect, so relax.
options.note: Presuming on grace while drifting is the very danger warned against.

options.text: Add extra rules to feel more secure.
options.note: The call is to attend to the gospel, not to build new laws.

options.text: Wait passively to see what happens.
options.note: The call is active attention, not passive waiting.

questions: Drifting happens slowly and quietly. Where might you be slipping away from Christ without noticing?
The writer says to pay closer attention to what we have heard. What practical habits keep the gospel in front of you week by week?
This is called so great a salvation. Have you let it grow small and familiar, or does its greatness still move you?
God confirmed the gospel with eyewitnesses and signs. How does the solid, attested nature of the good news steady your faith?
Who around you seems to be drifting, and how could you help them pay closer attention rather than slip away?

translationNotes.note: The verb rendered 'drift away' is a nautical and everyday image for slipping past or being carried off course; it stresses gradual, unintended loss rather than a sudden decision.
'The message spoken by angels' refers to the giving of the law at Sinai, which Jewish tradition associated with angelic mediation; 'binding' translates a term for what is legally firm and valid.
'Confirmed to us by those who heard Him' indicates the writer and readers received the gospel from eyewitnesses of the Lord, placing them in a second generation of believers.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Hebrews 2:1-4. No em dashes occurred in this passage. This is the first of the five warning passages; the strongest orthodox views are represented fairly. Sermon set to none; no expositor series confirmed this session.
